title: The Rebuild Gone Wrong
workOrder: WORK ORDER #214 — Station 4 came back from a weekend panel rebuild and "acts possessed." The contractor swears he landed every wire per the print. Nobody's run it since.
symptom: Something in this station is wired BACKWARDS or left unlanded — a different mistake each time you open the ticket. Power it up, watch what it does at rest, press the buttons, meter the test points, and find the miswire.

explains.STOP:swapped_no_nc: The rung is DEAD at rest — 24V at TP-1 but 0V at TP-2 — yet the instant you press and hold Stop, TP-2 goes HOT. A Stop button that has to be HELD to let the machine run is wired to its NO contact block instead of NC. Classic rebuild mistake: the contractor landed the wires on the wrong side of the contact block. Move them to the NC terminals — a Stop must be closed at rest so it can BREAK the circuit.
explains.START:swapped_no_nc: The machine started ITSELF the moment you powered up — nobody touched Start. That's a Start button wired to its NC block: it conducts at rest, feeds the coil immediately, and the seal-in latches it. Pressing Start actually OPENS the path (does nothing once latched). Any machine that self-starts on power-up is a serious safety event — this one gets fixed before the panel is closed.
explains.RUNC:swapped_no_nc: Control works perfectly — but the RUNNING light is lit while the machine sits stopped, and goes DARK when it runs. The lamp contact is on the wrong side of the block: an NC instead of the NO. The machine is fine; the indication is lying. Fix it anyway — an operator who trusts a backwards light will someday reach into a 'stopped' machine that's running.
explains.STPC:swapped_no_nc: The STOPPED light is dark at rest and lights up while the machine runs — backwards. Its contact was landed on the NO side instead of NC. Same lesson as the Running lamp: indication faults never stop production, so they never get reported — until the day someone believes the light.
explains.SEAL:broken_wire: Runs while you hold Start, dies the instant you release — the latch has no latch. During the rebuild the seal-in wire never got landed (or got landed on a dead terminal). 24V control checks fine everywhere else; the fingerprint is purely behavioral: hold-to-run. Land the seal-in contact in parallel with Start and it holds.
